speaker: interviewer
text: April 24, 1996. The survivor's is Sonja Waitzner. Her maiden name is Friedman. And this-- this is tape number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were, I would say, thousands of people standing outside. And we were put into formation. There was one woman from Brussels that I met in-- in the detention camp. And I was-- we became very close with her. And she was put in another group. And she ran over to us. She was beaten on her head so badly, so badly. The woman is still alive. She is almost deaf.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Trudi Rosenberg. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And she was-- she could not go with us. She wanted to be with Blanca and me. She always was my protector. But then I had a few of them because I was the youngest. We were put on a cattle train. And I start to be feverish. I was burning up and shaking like crazy from that cold, from that wet, cold air. And we went to Niederschlesien Liebau. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] When we arrived there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- one thing was better. There were no gas chambers. That is the only thing. The conditions were just as bad. But we went to work. That was now already going into November, very, very, cold. We had to stand appel two hours in the morning, two hours at night. In Auschwitz, we stand-- stood for many hours all the time. But there we went [GERMAN] kommando. That means we were to work in the fields. So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we got up 4:00 in the morning. We stood appel until 6:00 in the morning. Then we got whatever the gave us, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] barely to keep us alive. And then we started to work in the fields, and worked-- worked all day in the field. I don't remember what I did in the field, because it was already cold. I was all day out in the field. We were in the field working until-- from 6 o'clock in the morning until 6 o'clock in the evening. And then we walked back. Then we stood appel for two hours. And then we got our meal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The food consisted-- I would say we got like a thick soup. And it tasted like sawdust. That's the only thing I can thing of-- sawdust. We kept doing this day in, day out. And the snow started to fall. And one day, we walk back, and I become so weak that I feel like lying down and dying. Actually, the first time I want to die, lie down and die, because I turn around. I said, I cannot escape. I'm in Germany. I'm too weak to go anywhere. I cannot continue anymore. I'm too weak. I want to die. And I want to lie down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I said to myself, what am I? Crazy? No, I'm not going to die. Let them die. I'm going to live. And I pulled myself together. And I get up, and I continue walking. Luckily for us, the snow kept coming. What are they going to do with us in the field? So there was other work to be done.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to work in a factory that made snow chains for the tanks, for the Wehrmacht. And I lifted 100 kilo chains to solder links, 12 hours a day-- no, not a day, from 6 o'clock at night to 6:00 in the morning, night shift. Two hours appell in the morning-- in the evening before He went to work and two hours in the morning when we got back. I was so weak. I was so weak. But at least I was no longer outside all day. I was indoors in a factory. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And that went on until the liberation. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I-- I tell you something. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I cannot believe myself that I lived all this. I have-- my people and I, we, the survivors, have gone through so much. And I went only through one year-- only through one year. I'm going to tell you a little story. I know somebody from Brussels that lives now in Flushing, Queens, Herman Hollar. He lectures-- he was in Auschwitz a few years. He lectures a lot about the Holocaust in synagogues and also in universities.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- they I asked him to make a tape of one day in Auschwitz. And that was put into a time capsule in Yad Vesham in Jerusalem to be taken out in 100 years from now. And you know how it started out? How do you describe a day in Auschwitz when a day is a year? I spent my life, my youth, my childhood trying to survive that Nazi regime. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: December-- May 8, 1945. No, May 6, 1945, we suddenly didn't see the Nazis anymore. And we didn't know what happened. We didn't know anything. But we were afraid to go out. We were in Germany. And what do you think, May 8, suddenly the Russians come. We were liberated the last day of the war-- not a day before. We didn't know it was the end of the war even. The Russians came. Oh, the Russians came in. And that was the most memorable day I can remember, because among the Russians was a high officer, a handsome, tall man. And he addressed us in Jewish. And we couldn't believe it. He said to us, I've liberated came after camp. And I've sworn by the God Almighty-- and he threw his revolver out and held it up in the air-- I swore by the God Almighty that I will avenge what happened to my people. And the whole came dissolved into tears. Everybody was crying. It was the first time that ever cried in all the time that I was incarcerated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- the happiness, it was so great. It was so wonderful. I always dreamt, even through the war when I was not captured yet, I dreamt to own one loaf of bread for myself-- only a loaf of bread. That's all I always wanted, a loaf of bread for myself. The war ended, I couldn't eat two slices. We all started to have diarrhea. But we were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what do you think happened to me before we went transported by the Red Cross? A Russian soldier came into my-- our barrack. And he ran after me into my bunk. He wanted to rape me. And I screamed. And what do you think? My people, a woman took him into her bed. He was drunk. And she cuddled him. And he fell asleep. And she saved me. I said, those are my liberators? That's all I needed yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were-- we were-- we took the train to Prague. And we were there also in-- in a Kazerne-- in an army, sort of an army depot. It was an enormous place with cots. They put up cots for hundreds and hundreds of people. And I was free. I immediately started to improve. I started to eat. I couldn't wait-- I was there three weeks. I couldn't wait to come back to Brussels. By the time I went back to Brussels, it was four weeks later. I went to the place where my brothers were hiding. And the woman, [? Anna, ?] she told me where they were. I went to see my brothers. And I didn't want to go to my father. I wanted them to prepare him for my return, because he had already a heart condition. And when he saw me-- the joy of the reunion of my brothers and my father and I, it was just so wonderful. From that time on, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when my father came to America, if I didn't see him every day, I certainly talked to him every day. I adored him. We-- I loved him very dearly. My brothers were so devoted to him. They did so much good for him that I never knew until after he died. It was just great. I-- I will say, however, my father said, if Sonja doesn't return, I'll get myself a gun. I'll go to Germany. And I kill, shoot the first woman I see. And then I don't care what happens to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, Papa, you would do that? You would have done that? He said, yes, I would have. What had happened to your father in the meantime?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my father, I forgot to say, my father was let out from Mechlin. I forgot to tell that story, because at the time that was the last transport out of Belgium. And my father, they let out because he was fairly sick. They let out a few people. And I remember my father looking at me when he left, he was so devastated. And he stayed safe until the return, which was a year later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Blanca?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blanca returned with me. Blanca was very sick too. She had dysentery. Oh! She was bad. I thought she would never make it. It was bad, bad. Then in Brussels, I was-- a woman that was with us-- with me in the concentration camp, she told me her nephew is coming, who was in the-- in the American army, originally from Germany, Dortmund. And she came up where I worked and introduced me. And it was like love at first sight. He was in the American Air Force, in the intelligence service.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me their--

speaker: interviewee
text: I fell--

speaker: interviewer
text: --names

speaker: interviewee
text: Morris Waitzner, who later became my husband. I saw him only three times. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 19-- December 1946, I went to America. But I have to say that when I was liberated, I came back to Belgium. It was the first time that I was free and young. And I was going crazy with the joy of life. I didn't know what it means to be free. I didn't know what it meant to be young. I always had to work and try to survive. Work was nothing, but try to survive and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just survive. I was never young-- until I was 18 and 1/2 years old. And then I was irrepressible. I had friends galore. And those were mostly the children of survivors that I knew. I knew the survivors and I met the children, and the nephews, and so forth. And we had a club, a Jewish club, in Brussels. And we went out. And my brother and-- went out with Gertha and me. And we went dancing. And we went to coffee houses. And we had such a good time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was in love with love. I hadn't lived yet. And therefore, my marriage was much too fast. I fell in love with love. How can you-- my father said, you're not going to America. I said, so what? So I'll wait until I'm 21. So he tried to inquire who the family was. And then he did not want to hold me back. He let me go. And on the ship I turned 20. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to the United States. And as a fiance, I-- on a visiting visa, I had to three months. And I got married. And I was happy. And then suddenly, I got sick. I had tuberculosis. It came from the war yet. I had tuberculosis, and I had to go to the National Jewish Hospital of Denver, Colorado.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what do you think? There were a number of young people in the 20s all from Europe, all from concentration camps, all with tuberculosis. I was there for nine months. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I covered. And I came back. And I went to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father never knew. He was not in America yet. My father never knew. I never let him know. I wrote him letters to Belgium as if I would be in-- in New York, because we always tried to sort of save our father, all of us, all three. I never let him know that I had to tuberculosis. So I wrote him fictitious letters of a life that I didn't even lead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, got well. And then started to learn English well. And I used to go to the library, the-- the public library in New York and get French books. And after two years Morris said, so how long are you going to read French? How about starting English? So I started to read English. And I still love to read. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 years later, I had a son. He was the pride and joy of our life. He was just a wonderful kid, a normal kid. He had friends galore. He went to school. He was bright. He was skipped from second to fourth grade. He had a mathematical mind. He went to sleep away camp every summer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had a lot of fun. He had-- he had friends. He loved sports. I saw absolutely nothing wrong, until he started to become in his teens. After he left college, he became different. And I couldn't put my-- I couldn't quite understand. Then he went away to college. And the world fell apart. And he escaped into drugs. He couldn't cope.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I finally took him to a psychiatrist. And while he talks to him, and then he went, oh. I said-- suddenly I said to the psychiatrist, doctor, tell me, is he schizophrenic? He said, yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My world fell apart all over again. But I am really a strong person. And I tried very much to get him help. But he refused. And after a while, he didn't even want to leave the house. Now, I also have to say that my husband is a manic depressive. And I didn't know about that. I really didn't know what was happening either, because he became very, very hyper. Very intelligent, he's an accountant, a CPA, learned, became very hyper, very hard to live with, and then went into a total depression. It was so bad that he had to be hospitalized with shock treatments-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not once, but a few times. And they gave him medication. And that really helped, was great.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after a while, I saw a change in him. And I said, Morris, you're not taking the medication anymore? He says, no, I'm off it. I'm not going to take it anymore. And the same cycle began. So between trying to get help for my son-- You didn't give me your-- his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: The name of your son.

speaker: interviewee
text: Steven Mark Waitzner, Steven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So you were trying to get help--

speaker: interviewee
text: For my son, and he refused to get help. And my husband in that state was really of no help. If anything, he was a hindrance, because I really needed to call the police to get him out, to get him help. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when he heard that, he nixed the whole thing. It became so bad that finally my brother Leon said-- he pulled me out of it. And I went to live with him for six months. He lives on Long Island. You're going to meet him later. Norbert is not coming. That's not for him. I lived with my brother and sister-in-law and the kids. I lived with them for six months. I wanted to return, but there was no return, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until finally Morris had to go back into the hospital. He went to work and took care of Steven. Morris had to go back to the hospital. And that freed me to get help for Steven. That's when I stepped in. And he has been-- he has not been home since. He's now living in an adult home. Morris of-- with whom I'm separated-- by whom I'm separated lives in a senior citizen home in Long Beach, near the beach. He's quite well. He's on medication now. He's 82 years old. He's just fine. My son is about 15 minutes away from him. I go there every Saturday, pick up my son, pick up Morris. We all go together for lunch. And we go out together. I take my son on vacation by myself. And I give my son a good time when we're together. There no crying, nothing. We just have a good time together. And this is really my life now. I have been on the job for 30 years as a bookkeeper. I work there part time. I work now two days a week. I love it there. I really like there. This is a home for me. I'm home. I-- I tell them I come in. I tell them I don't come in. They're glad when the come in. 30 years is a long time. My son and I have a wonderful relationship. We talk only three times a day. He calls me Mutzi Putz. So how are you [? Kinderlei? ?] He says to me. He's cute. We have a good time together. He's happy. He does, however, tell me, Mom, I cannot live without you. And that hurts. But if something happens to me, I have two fabulous nieces, Renee and Linda, whom you're going to meet. Should something happened to me, they will take over. They grew up together. They're very close. They love my son. And he loves them. Finally, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I want to say that I'm glad in a way I did this. Maybe, perhaps it will help me to attain some peace of mind and tranquility. To some extent I have it. But maybe having spoken from-- sort of bared my soul somewhat will add to it. I also will want to say, finally, that I hope Israel lives forever in peace. And as a survivor, I want to say never again. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is my mother Sali Friedman. On the right is my father Chaim Friedman. This photo was taken in 1920, an engagement picture in Skalat, Poland. By the way, my mother died in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't know when and where, do you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was deported in 1941 to Poland. OK. On the left is Norbert, my brother. He's five years old. That is me, Sonja, three years old. And that is Leon on the right side, seven years old, my brothers and I in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1929. This is a court order from Vienna dated July 11, 1938, a notice of eviction to my father to evict us from our apartment in Vienna by the 31st of July at noontime, because the Aryan in the building do not wish to reside was non-Aryans. The superintendent of our building said to my father, Mr. Friedman, if all the Jews would be like you, then there would not be an anti-Semitism. My father said, every non-Jew has his honor Jew. There not as are many Jews living in Austria as there are honor Jews.
